issues.title: Exposes customer email (PII)
issues.problem: The query exposes customer email addresses, which is considered Personally Identifiable Information (PII).
issues.fix: Remove c.email from the SELECT clause.

issues.title: LEFT JOIN + WHERE on right table silently converts to INNER JOIN
issues.problem: The LEFT JOIN combined with the WHERE clause on the right table effectively converts the join to an INNER JOIN, potentially excluding customers with no completed transactions.
issues.fix: Use a CASE statement to handle the status condition within the SUM aggregation.

issues.title: GROUP BY missing c.tier — will error in strict SQL engines
issues.problem: The GROUP BY clause is missing c.tier, which will cause an error in strict SQL engines.
issues.fix: Include c.tier in the GROUP BY clause.

issues.title: No date filter — full table scan
issues.line:
FROM dim_customer c
LEFT JOIN fact_transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id
issues.problem: The query performs a full table scan on fact_transactions without any date filter, which can be inefficient.
issues.fix: Add a date filter to limit the rows scanned.

review.corrected_sql:
SELECT c.customer_name, c.tier,
       SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) as lifetime_value,
       COUNT(t.transaction_id) as total_orders
FROM dim_customer c
LEFT JOIN fact_transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_name, c.email, c.tier
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) > 1000
ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC;
review.summary: The query had security, correctness, and performance issues. The corrected version removes PII, fixes the join logic, includes all GROUP BY columns, and suggests adding a date filter for performance.

issues.title: Integer division
issues.problem: Integer division will truncate the decimal part, leading to incorrect failure rates for values less than 100%.
issues.fix: CAST(failed_count AS DECIMAL(10,2)) / total_count * 100 AS failure_rate

issues.title: Reference alias in same SELECT clause
issues.problem: Referencing an alias in the same SELECT clause can cause errors in most SQL engines.

issues.title: Correlated subquery
issues.line: (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fact_transactions f2 WHERE f2.status = 'FAILED' AND f2.transaction_date = t.transaction_date) AS failed_count
issues.problem: The correlated subquery re-scans the full table for each row in the outer query, which can be very inefficient.
issues.fix: Use a JOIN with a pre-aggregated subquery instead of a correlated subquery.

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the logic and calculations, making it harder to understand.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the purpose of each part of the query.

review.corrected_sql:
WITH failed_transactions AS (
  SELECT transaction_date, COUNT(*) AS failed_count
  FROM fact_transactions
  WHERE status = 'FAILED'
  GROUP BY transaction_date
)
SELECT t.transaction_date,
       COALESCE(f.failed_count, 0) AS failed_count,
       COUNT(*) AS total_count,
       COALESCE(f.failed_count, 0) * 100.0 / COUNT(*) AS failure_rate
FROM fact_transactions t
LEFT JOIN failed_transactions f ON t.transaction_date = f.transaction_date
GROUP BY t.transaction_date
ORDER BY t.transaction_date;
review.summary: The query had issues with integer division, referencing aliases in the same SELECT clause, and performance due to a correlated subquery. Additionally, it lacked comments for readability.

issues.title: Integer division truncation
issues.problem: Integer division truncates the result, leading to incorrect failure rates.
issues.fix: CAST(x AS DECIMAL) / total_count * 100 AS failure_rate

issues.title: PII exposure
issues.problem: Exposing customer email addresses without any filter or business need.

issues.title: Correlated subquery
issues.line: (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fact_transactions f2 WHERE f2.status = 'FAILED' AND f2.payment_method = t.payment_method AND f2.transaction_date = t.transaction_date) AS x
issues.problem: Correlated subquery re-scans the table for every row, leading to poor performance.
issues.fix: Use a window function or CTE to calculate the count.

issues.title: Cryptic column names
issues.problem: Column names 'x' and 'y' have no meaning, making the query hard to understand.

issues.title: Implicit join syntax
issues.problem: Implicit join syntax is less readable and more prone to errors.
issues.fix: Use explicit JOIN syntax: FROM fact_transactions t JOIN dim_customer c ON t.customer_id = c.customer_id

review.corrected_sql:
WITH failed_transactions AS (SELECT payment_method, transaction_date, COUNT(*) AS failed_count FROM fact_transactions WHERE status = 'FAILED' GROUP BY payment_method, transaction_date)
SELECT t.transaction_date,
       t.payment_method,
       COUNT(*) AS total_count,
       COALESCE(ft.failed_count, 0) AS failed_count,
       COALESCE(ft.failed_count, 0) * 1.0 / COUNT(*) * 100 AS failure_rate
FROM fact_transactions t
LEFT JOIN failed_transactions ft ON t.payment_method = ft.payment_method AND t.transaction_date = ft.transaction_date
GROUP BY t.transaction_date, t.payment_method
ORDER BY t.transaction_date;
review.summary: The query had critical issues with security and correctness, high performance issues, and medium readability issues. The corrected query addresses these issues and provides accurate failure rates without exposing PII.

issues.title: Missing STATUS = 'COMPLETED' filter
issues.problem: The query is counting all transactions as revenue, including failed ones, which is incorrect.

issues.title: Implicit JOIN syntax
issues.problem: Using implicit JOIN syntax can lead to confusion and potential errors. It's better to use explicit JOIN syntax.
issues.fix: FROM fact_transactions t JOIN dim_merchant m ON t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id

issues.title: ORDER BY ascending
issues.problem: The query orders by total_revenue in ascending order, which is incorrect for a 'top 10' use case. It should be descending.

issues.title: Lack of comments on complex logic
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the logic, which can make it harder to understand for other developers.
issues.fix: Add comments explaining the purpose of each part of the query.

review.corrected_sql:
-- Revenue report by merchant
SELECT m.merchant_name,
       SUM(t.amount) as total_revenue,
       COUNT(*) as txn_count
FROM fact_transactions t
JOIN dim_merchant m ON t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id
WHERE t.status = 'COMPLETED' AND t.transaction_date > '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY m.merchant_name
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 10;
review.summary: The query had issues with correctness and performance, and lacked readability. The corrected query now properly filters for completed transactions, uses explicit JOIN syntax, orders by descending revenue, and includes comments.
